transcript.text: Over the last few months, something very interesting has happened. Obviously, Meta has rolled out Andromeda and everything changed. Performance patterns shifted and right on Q, the internet filled up with videos saying things like, "The one campaign method is dead." In almost every one of those videos, they show the one campaign setup the wrong way. Then they criticize it while it's still outperforming much of the rest of their account. Hi, I'm Charlie. I created the original one campaign method and today I'm bringing you the one campaign method 2.0 and we're calling it Andromeda 1. In this video, I'm going to open up my ad account where I spend my money and I'm going to show you how to set up the Andromeda 1 correctly, how to move away from bloated, complicated accounts, and how to scale your business with more confidence and make more money. By the end of this video, you'll have the exact playbook you'll need for the whole year and your bank account and mental health will thank you. So instead of arguing theory, let me show you what this actually looks like inside of a real ad account. This is a higher spend account, which means we can reliably push multiple adsets out of learning. And that gives us something most advertisers never get. Signal clarity. So let's start with the structure breakdown. Adset one is our control. This is the benchmark. It contains a small number of proven ads. Not winners forever, just ads that are good enough to scale from and rely on. Then ad set two and three. These are the tests. Each of these contains a 322 flex ad with one purpose only. To improve the single worst ad inside of the control ad set, not to beat the whole campaign. Not to find a unicorn. Not to always graduate a post ID. Just to improve upon the weakest link. We are not testing to find winners to scale and abuse. We are testing to answer one simple question. Did this change make the campaign better or worse? Now, let's talk about how to judge a test. This is the most important part of Andromeda 1 and where almost every one of the experts on the internet completely screws it up. What we're measuring here is not individual CPA or individual rorowaz or which ad looks best. It's a volume conversation, not a vanity one. The only question we need to ask is did total revenue minus ad spent get better? This is what we call profit volume. And it's a simple custom metric that I have in every ad account. If yes, we can invest more. If no, the test is a loser. That's it. Real quick, if you like this video, please go ahead and like this video and subscribe so you don't miss anything else. And share it with all of your friends. YouTube loves that and it's how we help put fact to fiction so that everyone can see more success and less stress. Also, check out Disruptor Academy. We just launched a brand new course on Andromeda 1. Literally soup to nuts. Everything you need from setup to scale. Automated rules for low budgets, high budgets, literally everything you need to see more success and less stress. The link is right there. All right, that being said, let's get back to it. Budget behavior is the signal. Now, here's where this gets really powerful. When the test ads launch, we watch two things. Did the new ads get spent? If not, they're not good enough. The test fails immediately. Next, do the campaign performance improve or degrade? If spend shifts and performance improves, the test is a winner regardless of what any attribution has to say. If the spend shifts and performance on the campaign worsens, it's a bad test regardless of what any attribution has to say. It does not matter which ad won, which CPA was, what creative felt good. Only the business outcome matters. This structure gives us something that almost none of those experts and agency owners actually have certainty. Because we know exactly what changed, when it changed, and how it impacted the system, we can say with confidence whether that change had a positive or negative impact on the account and our business. And here's the hidden upside, the cross channel effect. Often what happens is this. Facebook looks slightly worse, but total store volume will go up. The site converts better. Search and email drive more revenue. And returning customer revenue goes up, too, because we're driving more of the right people. We're buying customers, not just trying to sell products at whatever attributed margin is most sexy. According to the report, we're not trying to define success as stealing credit for the last click. And this is the difference between optimizing ads as performance marketer and being a growth marketer scaling a business. This structure, by the way, also quietly solves ad fatigue. What most people call ad fatigue is usually just bad campaign management. It's operator error. In Andromeda 1, winners rarely have competition. And when they do, it's intentional. And we know immediately if that competition is good or bad. So performance decay isn't mysterious. It's measurable. It's avoidable. And sometimes it happens on purpose. And that's totally fine. If we're replacing somebody on the team with somebody better, that's a good thing. What Andromeda 1 is actually optimizing for where similarity beats scale. To really understand Andromeda 1, you have to understand what it's actually trying to do because it's not optimizing ads. The entire purpose of Andromeda 1 is to increase the volume of high quality data flowing into the platform so that the system can find more and more similar people, not more hot pockets of different folks, more people who look alike, who behave alike, who move through the customer journey in your business in very similar ways. Most ad accounts do the opposite. They try to sell a thousand products to a thousand different people who all buy once and disappear. that creates messy data, inconsistent behavior, fragile performance, and nothing compounds. Plus, managing CRO and email and all the other stuff is a complete nightmare. Andromeda 1 flips that entirely. Instead, we focus on a small number of ideal customer avatars who all behave similarly, buy multiple times, and move through the funnel in the same way. Then we go get thousands of them. And when the platform starts getting higher transaction volume from users who behave similarly, something powerful happens. The data gets cleaner, which means better prediction, better delivery, better future matching, better job at getting the right ad to the right person. And it does so cheaper with greater reach at lower CAC. And this doesn't just help ads. It unlocks scale everywhere. Search traffic converts better. Email converts better. SMS converts better. Creative insights get clearer. Your entire customer journey improves end to end because you stabilized the first step. Because now we're not optimizing a channel. We're training the entire system to find our best people. And this singularity and similarity in the customer journey is why the value of our efforts and the quality of it will always be more important than the volume of it. It's also where Andromeda changed everything. And I couldn't be more happy about it. When ads last longer and collect more data, the machine doesn't just optimize, it learns. It learns which ads to show to which person in which order, at which moment. And Andromeda is better at this than anything that has ever existed before. And it's easier to program than any algorithm has ever been. And that's because it's no longer obsessive being the last touch. It's not trying to take credit. It's not trying to steal attribution. It's not handing out a cup of water at mile 25 and claiming that's why somebody finished the marathon. Old performance marketing was all about one question. Who gets credit for the finish? Andromeda and Meta at a larger scale doesn't care about that anymore. It's trying to do something way more important, something much bigger. It's trying to get more people to start the marathon. Everything now is about incremental lift. That means more right people entering the journey, more people behaving similarly, more predictable outcomes downstream. And that is exactly why launching a ton of ads that all launch at the bottom of the funnel and just spamming it breaks the system. When you launch tons of ads and they all chase the same last click credit, you create chaotic customer journeys, different hooks, different promises, different motivations, which means you have a whole ton of different customer journey potentials to have to optimize for. The machine can't tell which ad to show to which person or in which sequence because everyone looks different and some ads that were here yesterday might not be there tomorrow. And when users don't look or behave similarly, the data gets noisy. The learning slows, the system never stabilizes. You're not scaling a business. You're just trying to make a buck today so you don't go out of business. And you stay stuck there. This is the difference between hunters and farmers. Hunters being performance marketers measure success by rorowaz. They ask, "Did this ad return today? What's my cost right now? Did this work immediately?" They live and die by shortterm performance. And you see this from all of the big YouTubers out there. That's what they're focused on. Every day is a hunt. Miss today and they panic tomorrow. However, farmers, growth marketers, measure success by total volume of profit. They ask, did this increase the amount of money the business made? Did this make the system stronger? Is momentum easier to maintain than it was before? Do we have a higher confidence what's going to happen tomorrow, next week, and next month? Because the better we get at this, the less work it takes to keep growing. It's not more effort, it's less. Andromeda is really smart. And it only stays smart if we don't spend all of our time and energy keeping it stupid. Now, I hope you're enjoying watching this video as much as I enjoyed making it. And I want to take a moment and just say thank you for watching it. Go ahead and smash the like button, subscribe to the channel if you haven't already, ring the bell so you don't miss any future videos, and take a moment to join the Disruptor Academy where you can get for free all the assistance that you need, all the things that we talk about in this video. There's a link right there. All right, with that being said, let's get back to it. When we stop fragmenting data, stop forcing chaos, and stop chasing last touch wins, we let the system learn deeply. And once it does, growth compounds with less input, not more. One farmer can feed a thousand hunters. Andromeda one is farming. It's saying, "Let's train the system on a small set of ideal customer avatars. Let's get thousands of them. Let's let learning compound and let every channel improve as a result." That's how real scale happens. That's how real businesses are built. That's how empires are made. That's the difference between somebody who is good at making a sale and somebody who's good at buying customers, a performance marketer and a growth expert. Now, the constraint of budget, there's a reality we have to respect. If your budget is low, you don't get infinite data. And if you can't get more than one adset out of learning, you can't force a mature structure. So, Andromeda 1 can bend to that need. Which brings us to the low budget strategy. When budget is constrained, our job isn't to outenineer the platform. The job is to maximize data quality inside of the smallest possible structure. This is the lowbudget rule and it is not negotiable. If you can't reliably get one adset out of learning, everything lives in one adset. That's not a compromise. That's the correct strategy. And I see a lot of people doing that at mass scale, complaining it doesn't work, but it's because it's only designed for a lowbudget setup. one campaign, one ad set, and a handful of 322 flex ads. We don't force isolation. We don't use premature structures. We don't have a lot of exclusions and we don't base things on rorowaz. The goal here is simple. Increase transaction volume until the budget can support more signal separation. Let's get to the place where we can afford to have multiple adsets so we can set up in a mature ad account. Once volume increases, the data stabilizes, the similarity increases and predictability improves and we can go to bigger audiences and we can scale further. And now naturally you can afford to graduate into control adsets and test adsets. But remember, you're always scaling everything inside of that one campaign. And each campaign that you run needs to have a very specific business objective. This is clean experimentation. Not because you wanted complexity, not because you're lazy, but because the account demands one specific setup versus another. And it earned that growth. This is why Andromeda 1 scales so well. It adapts to low budgets and to high budgets and everything in between because ultimately what you're doing is you're programming the machine to do one job very well with a clear delineation of success and a very easy way to measure every change in the impact on your business and you never have to change that philosophy. So now that you understand what Andromeda 1 actually is, it's not a tactic, it's not a hack, it's not a structure you copy from a screenshot. Andromeda 1 is a decision to stop being a hunter and start being a farmer. To stop asking, did this work today and start asking did this make my system smarter? Did this increase the volume of similar customers? Is growth easier to maintain than it was before? Ultimately, can we spend more money tomorrow? Because when the answer to those questions is yes, profit follows automatically. If you keep your structure simple, you let ads live long enough to learn, and you focus on similarity rather than chaos, you increase budget only when the system earns it, Andromeda will do what it's designed to do. It will compound. And compounding interest is the most powerful force in the entire universe. And the less you interfere, the better it gets. This is why people think the one campaign method doesn't work. They keep their system stupid. They interrupt learning. They chase short-term validation. and they try a thousand things in a thousand different places. Then they blame the system and say, "Well, the platform works differently for everybody." No, it doesn't. It's software. Excel works the same for everybody. So does Meta. Once Andromeda starts working, the fastest way to destroy it is by touching the budget at the wrong time, by launching more ads, by doing everything you can to destabilize the win that you have. If you can increase the budget, don't do anything else. Now, we talked about the structure. What we need to understand also is the best kind of ad. And in this video, I'm going to show you exactly what you should be doing, the greatest kind of ad you can build, and how to best weaponize machine learning and data. Also, down here, you'll find links to disruptor academy. I think you should join it.
